title: The Compounding Cost: How Differential Privacy and Model Compression Jointly Amplify Fairness Degradation
description: We show that combining DP-SGD training with model compression (pruning/quantization) produces super-additive fairness degradation for underrepresented subgroups — worse than the sum of each technique's individual impact. We propose FairPrune-DP, a worst-group-aware pruning algorithm that breaks this feedback loop by using per-subgroup Fisher information to protect minority-relevant features during compression.
motivation: In real-world ML deployment, models are often trained with differential privacy for legal compliance and then compressed for efficient inference. Two independent research lines show that DP-SGD and compression each amplify bias against minority groups, but their interaction has never been studied. Since both mechanisms preferentially degrade features with low signal-to-noise ratio — which are exactly the features encoding minority-group information — the fairness costs may compound super-additively, creating a hidden tax on underrepresented populations that current analyses fail to capture.
proposed_approach: Three-part contribution: (1) Systematic empirical measurement of the compounding fairness effect across datasets (CelebA, UTKFace, imbalanced CIFAR-10), architectures (ResNet-18, MobileNetV2), privacy budgets (epsilon in {1,2,4,8,inf}), and compression rates (30-90% sparsity, INT8/INT4 quantization). We define a 'compounding ratio' CR = Delta_DC / (Delta_D + Delta_C) where Delta measures worst-group accuracy drop. (2) Mechanistic analysis showing DP-trained models have systematically smaller weights for minority-relevant features, making them more vulnerable to magnitude-based pruning. (3) FairPrune-DP: replace global importance scores with worst-group Fisher information s_i^fair = min_g E[(dL/dw_i)^2 | group g], ensuring features critical to any subgroup are retained during pruning.

related_work.title: Differential Privacy Has Disparate Impact on Model Accuracy
related_work.relationship: Establishes that DP-SGD disproportionately degrades accuracy for underrepresented groups. We extend this by showing compression compounds the effect.

related_work.title: Characterising Bias in Compressed Models
related_work.authors: Sara Hooker, Nyalleng Moorosi, Gregory Clark, Samy Bengio, Emily Denton
related_work.relationship: Shows compression amplifies bias via Compression Identified Exemplars. We show the effect is worse when starting from DP-trained models.

related_work.title: Disparate Impact in Differential Privacy from Gradient Misalignment
related_work.authors: Maria S. Esipova, Atiyeh Ashari Ghomi, Yaqiao Luo, Jesse C. Cresswell
related_work.relationship: Identifies gradient misalignment from clipping as the root cause of DP's disparate impact. Our mechanistic analysis builds on this by showing how misalignment creates a downstream vulnerability to pruning.

related_work.title: CompLeak: Deep Learning Model Compression Exacerbates Privacy Leakage
related_work.authors: Na Li, Yansong Gao, Hongsheng Hu, Boyu Kuang, Anmin Fu
related_work.relationship: Shows compression increases membership inference vulnerability. We study whether this privacy amplification disproportionately affects minority subgroups.

related_work.title: Defending Against Membership Inference Attacks on Iteratively Pruned Deep Neural Networks
related_work.authors: Jing Shang, Jian Wang, Kailun Wang, Jiqiang Liu, Nan Jiang, Md Armanuzzaman, Ziming Zhao
related_work.relationship: Proposes WeMem defense for pruned models. Our work provides complementary analysis of how DP interacts with pruning-induced memorization.

related_work.title: Integrating Fairness and Model Pruning Through Bi-level Optimization
related_work.authors: Yucong Dai, Gen Li, Feng Luo, Xiaolong Ma, Yongkai Wu
related_work.relationship: Proposes joint fairness-pruning optimization for standard models. We address the distinct challenge of pruning DP-trained models where the weight landscape is fundamentally different.

related_work.title: DPQuant: Efficient and Differentially-Private Model Training via Dynamic Quantization Scheduling
related_work.authors: Yubo Gao, Renbo Tu, Gennady Pekhimenko, Nandita Vijaykumar
related_work.relationship: Studies quantization during DP training for efficiency. We study post-training compression's fairness impact, a complementary concern.

related_work.title: Disparate Vulnerability to Membership Inference Attacks
related_work.authors: Bogdan Kulynych, Mohammad Yaghini, Giovanni Cherubin, Michael Veale, Carmela Troncoso
related_work.relationship: Establishes that MIA vulnerability varies across subgroups. We extend this analysis to the DP + compression pipeline.

hypothesis: The fairness degradation (measured by worst-group accuracy drop and accuracy gap between subgroups) from combining DP-SGD training with magnitude-based pruning is super-additive — the compounding ratio CR > 1 — because both mechanisms share a vulnerability pathway: preferential degradation of features with low signal-to-noise ratio, which encode minority-group information. FairPrune-DP, by using worst-group Fisher importance for pruning decisions, can reduce the fairness gap by 20-50% at comparable compression ratios.
success_criteria: Compounding ratio CR > 1.2 consistently across at least 2 datasets and multiple (epsilon, sparsity) configurations

Mechanistic evidence: weight magnitude distributions for minority-relevant features are systematically lower in DP-trained models, and pruning overlap analysis shows >60% of additionally-pruned weights (DP vs non-DP) correspond to minority-relevant features
FairPrune-DP reduces worst-group accuracy gap by >= 20% relative to standard magnitude pruning of DP models at the same compression ratio
MIA success rate disparity across subgroups increases after compression, more so for DP-trained models
Results hold for both unstructured and structured pruning at sparsity >= 50%
